questions.question: Which statement best defines a pure bluff?
options: Betting a hand with almost no chance to win if called, relying on folds
Betting a strong made hand to get called by worse
Checking a draw to see a free card
Calling a river bet with a marginal hand

explanations.A: Correct. A pure bluff has little or no showdown equity and wins almost exclusively through fold equity.
explanations.B: That describes a value bet, which wants calls from worse hands, not a bluff.
explanations.C: Checking to realize equity is a passive line, not a bluff.
explanations.D: Calling is a bluff-catch or value decision, not a bluff.

questions.question: A pure bluff makes money in exactly one way. What is it?
options: You always improve on the river
Your opponent folds a better hand
The board pairs your kicker
You get to showdown cheaply

explanations.A: If you reliably improved you would not be bluffing — that would be a draw or made hand.
explanations.B: Correct. A pure bluff has no meaningful backup equity, so its entire profit comes from making a better hand fold.
explanations.C: Catching a pair gives the hand showdown value, which is not the pure-bluff win condition.
explanations.D: Reaching showdown with air usually loses; bluffs need folds, not cheap showdowns.

questions.question: What distinguishes a semi-bluff from a pure bluff?
options: A semi-bluff is only made on the river
A semi-bluff never wants a fold
A semi-bluff can win the pot by improving even when called
A semi-bluff is always larger than a pure bluff

explanations.A: A river bet has no more cards to come, so it cannot be a semi-bluff; semi-bluffs occur on the flop or turn.
explanations.B: A semi-bluff is happy to win immediately via a fold; it simply also has backup equity.
explanations.C: Correct. A semi-bluff bets a draw, so it wins immediately if villain folds and can still win by completing when called.
explanations.D: Sizing is unrelated to whether a bet is a semi-bluff or a pure bluff.

questions.question: Fold equity refers to:
options: The rake taken by the house
The size of the blinds in the pot
The raw strength of your hand at showdown
The value you gain from the probability your opponent folds

explanations.A: Rake reduces winnings but has nothing to do with fold equity.
explanations.B: Blinds are dead money already in the pot, not fold equity.
explanations.C: Showdown strength is separate from how often a bet makes opponents fold.
explanations.D: Correct. Fold equity is the expected value contributed by the chance the opponent folds to your aggression.

questions.question: Against which opponent do you have the LEAST fold equity?
options: A calling station who rarely folds once involved
A tight player who folds to most aggression
A short stack scared to bust the tournament
A player whose range is mostly air

explanations.A: Correct. A calling station folds infrequently, so your bluffs rarely take the pot down — minimal fold equity.
explanations.B: Tight players fold often, giving you high fold equity.
explanations.C: A scared short stack often folds more, increasing fold equity.
explanations.D: An air-heavy range folds to pressure, giving you plenty of fold equity.

questions.question: You bet $50 into a $100 pot as a pure bluff with no equity if called. What fold frequency do you need to break even?

explanations.A: 25% corresponds to a quarter-pot bet, not a half-pot bet.
explanations.B: Correct. Break-even fold % = bet / (bet + pot) = 50 / (50 + 100) = 1/3 ≈ 33%.
explanations.C: 50% is the break-even for a pot-sized bet, not a half-pot bet.
explanations.D: 67% is the defender's minimum defense frequency versus a half-pot bet, not the bluffer's break-even.

questions.question: A pure bluff of pot size (bet = pot) needs the opponent to fold at least:
options: 33% of the time
40% of the time
50% of the time
67% of the time

explanations.A: 33% is the threshold for a half-pot bet, which risks less.
explanations.B: 40% is the break-even for a 2x-pot overbet, not a pot-sized bet.
explanations.C: Correct. Break-even = bet / (bet + pot) = P / (P + P) = 1/2 = 50%.
explanations.D: 67% would apply to roughly a 2x-pot bet, not a pot-sized bet.

questions.question: Which flop is generally the best single-barrel bluffing texture for the preflop raiser heads-up?
options: 9♥ 8♥ 7♠ (very coordinated)
6♥ 5♥ 4♥ (monotone and connected)
J♠ T♠ 9♦ (wet, draw-heavy)
A♣ 7♦ 2♠ (dry, ace-high)

explanations.A: A coordinated 9-8-7 hits a caller's range hard and offers many draws to continue, lowering fold equity.
explanations.B: A monotone connected board is loaded with made hands and draws that happily continue.
explanations.C: A wet J-T-9 connects with many calling hands and draws, so bluffs get called or raised often.
explanations.D: Correct. A dry ace-high board favors the raiser's range and gives the caller few strong hands or draws, so c-bet bluffs fold out plenty of air.

questions.question: Telling a 'credible story' with a bluff means:
options: Choosing a line that matches hands your value range would also play this way
Betting a random amount so you are unpredictable
Only bluffing when you hold the nuts
Talking to convince the opponent you are strong

explanations.A: Correct. A believable bluff represents a plausible value hand, so the opponent cannot easily rule out that you are strong.
explanations.B: Random sizing often breaks the story and tips off observant opponents.
explanations.C: If you held the nuts it would be a value bet, not a bluff.
explanations.D: Table talk is unreliable; the story is told through sizing and line, not chatter.

questions.question: Holding the A♠ on a final board of K♠ 9♠ 4♠ 2♦ when bluffing is valuable because it:
options: Guarantees you win the pot if called
Removes nut-flush combinations from the opponent's range
Means you already have a made flush
Has no effect on the opponent's holdings

explanations.A: Holding one ace does not make a hand; if called by any made hand you typically lose.
explanations.B: Correct. The A♠ blocks the nut flush, so the opponent holds fewer of the strongest hands and folds more often.
explanations.C: A single A♠ is not a flush; you would need the ace plus the board's spades in your own hand.
explanations.D: Card removal directly reduces the combos of strong spade hands villain can have.

questions.question: Minimum defense frequency (MDF) describes:
options: How often the bettor should bluff
The percentage of pots you should win
The fraction of range a defender must continue with to stop any-two-card bluffs from auto-profiting
How often you should fold to a preflop 3-bet

explanations.A: How often to bluff is the bettor's value-to-bluff question, not MDF.
explanations.B: MDF is about defending against bets, not a target win rate.
explanations.C: Correct. MDF = pot / (pot + bet); defending at least this fraction makes a pure any-two bluff unprofitable.
explanations.D: MDF is a general defense concept, not a specific preflop rule.

questions.question: Facing a half-pot bet, a defender's minimum defense frequency is approximately:

explanations.A: 33% is the bluffer's break-even fold target versus a half-pot bet — the complement of MDF.
explanations.B: 50% MDF corresponds to a pot-sized bet, not a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: 80% MDF applies to a quarter-pot bet, which is much smaller.
explanations.D: Correct. MDF = pot / (pot + bet) = 1 / (1 + 0.5) ≈ 67%, so you defend about two-thirds of your range.

questions.question: Why can a semi-bluff be profitable even when villain folds less than a pure bluff would require?
options: Because the draw's equity adds value on the times you get called
Because the rules give semi-bluffs a discount
Because semi-bluffs are always checked
Because draws never lose at showdown

explanations.A: Correct. A semi-bluff wins when villain folds and retains chances to win by completing when called, lowering the required fold frequency.
explanations.B: There is no rule discount; the advantage is purely the added equity.
explanations.C: A semi-bluff is by definition a bet or raise, not a check.
explanations.D: Draws often miss and lose; they merely add some equity, not a guarantee.

questions.question: A flush draw on the flop has 9 outs. Using the rule of 4, roughly what is its chance to complete by the river?
options: About 18%
About 36%
About 24%
About 50%

explanations.A: 18% is the rule-of-2 estimate for a single card (turn only), not flop-to-river.
explanations.B: Correct. Rule of 4: 9 × 4 ≈ 36% (the precise figure is about 35%) to hit by the river.
explanations.C: 24% understates it; that is closer to two overcards.
explanations.D: 50% greatly overstates a nine-out flush draw's two-card equity.

questions.question: When is giving up on a bluff usually the best decision?
options: Whenever you have already invested chips, keep firing to justify them
Only before the flop
When fold equity has disappeared and your story no longer makes sense
Never — good players always triple barrel

explanations.A: Already-invested chips are sunk cost; chasing them with more bluffs compounds losses.
explanations.B: Bluffs and give-ups happen across all postflop streets, not just preflop.
explanations.C: Correct. If villain shows strength or your line stops representing value, more bluffs burn chips with little fold equity.
explanations.D: Reflexively triple-barreling without fold equity is a classic losing leak.

questions.question: Why are bluffs generally worse in multiway pots than heads-up?
options: The pot is too small to matter
Bluffing is against the rules multiway
Multiway pots have no fold equity at all
Each extra opponent is another player who can wake up with a hand and call

explanations.A: Multiway pots are usually larger, and size is not the core issue.
explanations.B: Bluffing is legal; it is simply less effective against multiple opponents.
explanations.C: There is still some fold equity multiway, just far less than heads-up.
explanations.D: Correct. You need everyone to fold, so the chance all opponents fold drops sharply with each added player.

questions.question: Why is bluffing typically easier in position than out of position?
options: Acting last lets you see the opponent's action before deciding
Out of position you can never bet
Position has no impact on bluff success
In position you are forced to bluff every hand

explanations.A: Correct. Acting last lets you bluff when weakness is shown and shut down when strength appears.
explanations.B: You can bet out of position; it is just harder to control the pot.
explanations.C: Position strongly affects information and pot control, which drive bluff EV.
explanations.D: Position is an advantage, not an obligation to bluff.

questions.question: All else equal, a larger pure-bluff bet relative to the pot:
options: Requires the opponent to fold less often to break even
Requires the opponent to fold more often to break even
Has no effect on the break-even fold frequency
Is always the most profitable choice

explanations.A: This reverses the math; larger bets need a higher fold frequency, not lower.
explanations.B: Correct. Break-even fold % = bet/(bet+pot), which rises with bet size, so bigger bluffs must succeed more often.
explanations.C: Bet size clearly changes the break-even fold frequency.
explanations.D: Bigger is not automatically better; profitability depends on how often the opponent folds.

questions.question: For a balanced pot-sized river bet, what value-to-bluff ratio makes a bluff-catcher indifferent?
options: 1:1 (equal value and bluffs)
3:1 (three value bets per bluff)
2:1 (two value bets per bluff)
1:2 (two bluffs per value bet)

explanations.A: Equal value and bluffs over-bluffs a pot-sized bet, making calls profitable.
explanations.B: 3:1 is the balanced ratio for a half-pot bet, not a pot-sized bet.
explanations.C: Correct. For a pot-sized river bet, bluffs are about 1/3 of the betting range — a 2:1 value-to-bluff ratio.
explanations.D: Bluffing more than you value bet is wildly exploitable.

questions.question: A 'scare card' that supports a turn or river barrel bluff is one that:
options: Helps the caller's range more than yours
Is always the lowest card in the deck
Never changes either player's range
Improves a range you can represent while missing the caller's continuing range

explanations.A: A card that helps the caller is the opposite of a scare card for you.
explanations.B: Low cards are usually blanks, not scare cards.
explanations.C: A blank that changes nothing gives little extra fold equity.
explanations.D: Correct. A card you can credibly represent that misses villain's range generates fold equity for a barrel.

questions.question: A 'stone-cold bluff' on the river is best described as betting with:
options: A hand that beats nothing in villain's calling range
The current best hand
Top pair top kicker
A flush draw with two cards to come

explanations.A: Correct. A stone-cold bluff has no showdown value and only wins if villain folds.
explanations.B: If you hold the best hand it is a value bet, not a bluff.
explanations.C: Top pair top kicker bets for value and protection, not as a bluff.
explanations.D: On the river there are no cards to come, so this cannot be a draw or semi-bluff.

questions.question: Which holding is the strongest semi-bluff candidate to raise on a 8♠ 7♠ 2♦ flop?
options: Q♣ Q♦ (overpair)
A♠ 9♠ (nut flush draw plus a gutshot)
3♥ 3♣ (small pair)
K♦ 2♣ (bottom pair)

explanations.A: An overpair is usually a value hand that prefers to bet/call rather than semi-bluff raise.
explanations.B: Correct. The nut flush draw plus gutshot has many outs and blocks strong spade hands, ideal for a semi-bluff raise.
explanations.C: A small pair has little equity to add and no draw — closer to a marginal call or fold.
explanations.D: Bottom pair is a weak made hand, not a strong semi-bluff with outs.

questions.question: Fold equity tends to be HIGHEST when:
options: Many players remain in the pot
The opponent has shown strength by raising
The opponent's range is capped and contains many weak hands
Stacks are extremely deep and the opponent is curious

explanations.A: More players means more chances someone holds a hand, lowering fold equity.
explanations.B: A raise signals strength and a range that continues, reducing fold equity.
explanations.C: Correct. A capped, weak range folds frequently to pressure, maximizing fold equity.
explanations.D: Deep stacks and curiosity often mean more calls, not more folds.

questions.question: You bet $25 into a $100 pot as a pure bluff. What fold frequency breaks you even?

explanations.A: 50% is the pot-sized threshold, far larger than a quarter-pot bet.
explanations.B: 25% corresponds to a one-third-pot bet, not a quarter-pot bet.
explanations.C: 33% is the half-pot threshold.
explanations.D: Correct. Break-even = bet/(bet+pot) = 25/125 = 20%.

questions.question: A 2x-pot overbet bluff with no equity if called needs the opponent to fold at least:

explanations.A: Correct. Break-even = bet/(bet+pot) = 2/(2+1) = 2/3 ≈ 67%.
explanations.B: 60% is the break-even for a 1.5x-pot bet.
explanations.C: 50% is the pot-sized threshold; an overbet needs more.
explanations.D: 75% corresponds to a 3x-pot bet, larger than 2x pot.

questions.question: On which river is a bluff most credible if you have been representing a flush draw?
options: A blank that pairs the bottom card
The card that completes the flush you were drawing to
A brick that changes nothing
An offsuit ace unrelated to your draws

explanations.A: A pairing blank doesn't complete the story you were telling about a flush draw.
explanations.B: Correct. The flush-completing card lets you credibly represent the very hand your line suggested, maximizing fold equity.
explanations.C: A brick fails to complete the draw you represented, so the story stalls.
explanations.D: An unrelated ace does not complete the flush you were drawing to.

questions.question: You call preflop, then on a Q-7-2 flop you check-call, check-call turn, and lead a big river bet. The most credible story for this line is:
options: A busted high-card bluff
Pure air with no plan
A slowplayed strong hand that woke up on the river
A missed flush draw

explanations.A: A busted bluff usually cannot credibly lead big after passively calling down.
explanations.B: Aimless air does not form a coherent narrative for a large river lead.
explanations.C: Correct. Check-call lines that suddenly lead the river can credibly represent a trap or a hand that just improved.
explanations.D: A missed flush draw has no showdown value and is a weaker story than a strong made hand for a value-style lead.

questions.question: On a J♥ T♥ 4♠ 2♥ 5♦ board, which blocker is BEST for a river bluff representing the nut flush?
options: The 2♦ in your hand
The A♠ in your hand
The 4♣ in your hand
The A♥ in your hand

explanations.A: The 2♦ blocks a low pair, not the nut flush combos.
explanations.B: The A♠ is the wrong suit and blocks no flush combos on this heart board.
explanations.C: The 4♣ blocks only a paired four, not the flushes you want to fold out.
explanations.D: Correct. Holding the A♥ removes the nut flush from villain's range, so your bluff faces fewer calls from the strongest hands.

questions.question: You c-bet a bluff on the flop and get check-raised by a tight, straightforward opponent. The standard play with pure air is to:
options: Fold, since the check-raise represents strength and your equity is low
Always 3-bet shove as a bigger bluff
Call to see another card with no plan
Slow-roll your air

explanations.A: Correct. Against a straightforward player a check-raise means strength; with no equity and no fold equity, folding is best.
explanations.B: Re-bluffing a tight player who just showed strength is high-risk and usually -EV.
explanations.C: Calling with air and no draw simply donates chips.
explanations.D: Slow-rolling is poor etiquette and irrelevant to the decision.

questions.question: The correct overall adjustment against a pure calling station is to:
options: Bluff much more because they fear aggression
Bluff far less and value bet thinner and bigger
Check every strong hand for deception
Only play premium hands and never bet

explanations.A: More bluffs against a non-folder loses money quickly.
explanations.B: Correct. Stations rarely fold, so cut bluffs and extract maximum value with wider, larger value bets.
explanations.C: Checking strong hands forfeits value the station would have paid off.
explanations.D: Only playing premiums leaves easy value on the table against a loose caller.

questions.question: You c-bet a bluff on K♦ 6♣ 3♠ and get called. Which turn card is the best second-barrel bluff card?
options: A 3, pairing the bottom card and changing little for you
A 6, pairing the board and helping villain's trips
An ace, which you can represent and which scares the caller's middling hands
A blank deuce that helps neither range

explanations.A: A 3 pairs the bottom and does little to scare the caller's range.
explanations.B: A pairing 6 helps the caller's sixes and rarely improves your story.
explanations.C: Correct. An ace adds a card to your representable range and pressures the caller's weak kings and pairs, generating fold equity.
explanations.D: A pure blank gives no extra fold equity over the flop.

questions.question: An open-ended straight draw has 8 outs. By the river (two cards), its approximate equity is:
options: About 17%
About 24%
About 45%
About 32%

explanations.A: 17% is the one-card (single street) estimate for 8 outs.
explanations.B: 24% understates the two-card equity of an open-ender.
explanations.C: 45% is closer to a 12-out combo draw, not a plain open-ender.
explanations.D: Correct. Rule of 4: 8 × 4 ≈ 32% (about 31.5% precisely) by the river.

questions.question: A 'polarized' betting range is composed of:
options: Strong value hands and bluffs, with few medium hands
Only medium-strength hands
Exclusively the nuts
A random mix unrelated to hand strength

explanations.A: Correct. A polarized range pairs strong value with bluffs, which is why large bets and overbets fit it well.
explanations.B: Medium hands belong to a merged or capped range, not a polarized one.
explanations.C: Betting only the nuts is too narrow and easily exploited by folding.
explanations.D: Polarization is defined precisely by hand strength, not randomness.

questions.question: Overbet bluffs (betting more than the pot) work best when:
options: Your range is capped at one pair
Your range is polarized and the board favors your strong hands
You hold only medium-strength hands
The opponent's range is uncapped and very strong

explanations.A: A capped range cannot credibly overbet, since you rarely hold the strongest hands.
explanations.B: Correct. Overbets leverage a polarized range with nutted hands and bluffs on boards where you can credibly hold the nuts.
explanations.C: Medium hands do not want to bloat the pot with an overbet.
explanations.D: If villain's range is uncapped and strong, an overbet finds too many calls.

questions.question: For a balanced half-pot river bet, the value-to-bluff ratio that makes a bluff-catcher indifferent is about:

explanations.A: 1:1 over-bluffs a half-pot bet.
explanations.B: 2:1 is the balanced ratio for a pot-sized bet.
explanations.C: Correct. Bluffs are about B/(P+2B) = 0.5/2 = 25% of the range, giving 3 value bets per bluff.
explanations.D: 3:2 is the balanced ratio for a 2x-pot overbet.

questions.question: A check-raise bluff out of position is most effective when:
options: Five players are still in the pot
The bettor only continuation-bets the nuts
You hold the absolute worst possible hand with no blockers
You can credibly represent strong hands and the bettor's c-bet range is wide and weak

explanations.A: A crowded multiway pot makes any bluff, including check-raises, far less effective.
explanations.B: If the bettor only c-bets the nuts, your raise gets called or re-raised by strength.
explanations.C: Bluffing without any blockers and the worst hand is the least efficient choice.
explanations.D: Correct. Check-raise bluffs punish wide, weak c-betting ranges while representing the nutted hands you can have.

questions.question: On the flop, a gutshot straight draw has 4 outs. Its approximate equity to hit by the river is:
options: About 16%
About 8%
About 25%
About 32%

explanations.A: Correct. Rule of 4: 4 × 4 ≈ 16% (about 16.5%) by the river.
explanations.B: 8% is roughly the single-card (one street) chance for a gutshot.
explanations.C: 25% overstates a four-out draw's two-card equity.
explanations.D: 32% is closer to an eight-out open-ender.

questions.question: Having a 'range advantage' on a flop means:
options: You always have the single best hand
Your overall range is stronger than the opponent's on that board
You have more chips than the opponent
You acted first preflop

explanations.A: Range advantage is about the whole range, not any one hand.
explanations.B: Correct. Range advantage means your distribution of hands fares better than villain's on that texture, supporting more betting and bluffing.
explanations.C: Stack size is not the same as range strength.
explanations.D: Acting order is position, not range advantage.

questions.question: A range is 'capped' when:
options: It has exactly four combinations
It contains only the nuts
It cannot contain the strongest possible hands
It is always all-in

explanations.A: Cap describes strength ceiling, not a fixed combo count.
explanations.B: Containing only the nuts is the opposite of capped — it is the uncapped extreme.
explanations.C: Correct. A capped range lacks the top of the distribution, making it vulnerable to large bets and overbet bluffs.
explanations.D: Being all-in is unrelated to whether a range is capped.

questions.question: On a 9♠ 8♠ 5♠ 2♣ 2♦ board, which hand is the best pure bluff to fire the river representing a flush?
options: 2♥ 3♥ (trip deuces — actually a value hand)
K♥ Q♥ (no spade)
7♣ 6♣ (busted straight, no spade)
A♠ K♦ (holds the nut-flush blocker)

explanations.A: Trip deuces is a made value hand, not a bluff.
explanations.B: K♥ Q♥ holds no spade and blocks none of villain's flushes.
explanations.C: A busted straight with no spade blocks nothing relevant to the flush you represent.
explanations.D: Correct. The A♠ blocks the nut flush, so villain holds the strongest flushes less often and folds more to your bluff.

questions.question: Compared with checking a flush draw, semi-bluff betting it adds value primarily by:
options: Creating fold equity on top of the draw's existing equity
Guaranteeing the flush completes
Removing the opponent's ability to ever have a hand
Reducing your own number of outs

explanations.A: Correct. Betting the draw can win immediately via folds while still retaining the chance to hit, combining two ways to win.
explanations.B: Betting does not change the odds of completing the draw.
explanations.C: Betting cannot remove villain's holdings; it only pressures them.
explanations.D: Your out count is unchanged by betting versus checking.

questions.question: A bluffer risks $80 to win a $160 pot (half-pot). The break-even fold frequency is closest to:

explanations.A: 40% would correspond to a two-thirds-pot bet.
explanations.C: 50% is the pot-sized threshold.
explanations.D: 67% is the defender's MDF here, not the bluffer's break-even.

questions.question: A 'donk bet' bluff is when the player who:
options: Checks and then calls
Raised preflop continues betting
Did not take the betting lead on the prior street leads into the previous aggressor
Folds out of turn

explanations.A: Checking and calling is passive, not a donk lead.
explanations.B: The preflop raiser continuing is a standard c-bet, not a donk bet.
explanations.C: Correct. A donk bet is an out-of-turn lead by the player who was not the previous aggressor, often used as a bluff on cards that favor the caller.
explanations.D: Folding out of turn is a procedural error, not a bluffing line.

questions.question: Floating the flop as a bluff plan means:
options: Checking back the nuts for value
Folding immediately to any bet
Raising all-in on the flop
Calling a flop bet with a weak hand intending to take the pot away on a later street

explanations.A: Checking back the nuts is a slowplay, not a float bluff.
explanations.B: Folding is the opposite of floating.
explanations.C: Shoving the flop is a different, more committed line.
explanations.D: Correct. A float calls the flop with little made-hand value, planning to bluff later when villain shows weakness.

questions.question: Fold equity in a semi-bluff all-in is most relevant because:
options: Folds let you win without needing to complete the draw
All-ins remove the value of your draw
Fold equity only matters preflop
Once all-in there are no more decisions, so folds cannot happen

explanations.A: Correct. The shove can win immediately if villain folds, and you still have draw equity when called — two ways to win.
explanations.B: An all-in does not erase the draw's equity when called.
explanations.C: Fold equity applies on every street, not just preflop.
explanations.D: When you shove, the opponent still chooses to call or fold, so fold equity exists.

questions.question: A successful triple-barrel bluff most depends on:
options: Random sizing on each street
A consistent story and runouts that favor your representable range
Holding the nuts the whole way
The pot staying small

explanations.A: Erratic sizing undermines the story and invites calls.
explanations.B: Correct. Three streets of pressure require a coherent narrative and cards that keep credibly representing strong hands.
explanations.C: Holding the nuts makes it value betting, not bluffing.
explanations.D: Triple barrels build a large pot; a small pot is not the goal.

questions.question: Why are paired boards (e.g., K♣ K♦ 5♠) often good single-barrel bluff spots heads-up?
options: Paired boards have the most draws
The board guarantees you a full house
The caller connects with fewer combos, so a bet folds out air frequently
Bluffing is impossible on paired boards

explanations.A: Paired dry boards typically have FEW draws, which is why they favor bluffs.
explanations.B: The board does not give you any specific hand.
explanations.C: Correct. Paired, dry boards miss most of the caller's range, giving a c-bet bluff plenty of fold equity.
explanations.D: Bluffing is very much possible — often profitable — on such textures.

questions.question: When choosing a river bluff, you ideally want a hand that blocks villain's calls and UN-blocks their folds. That means you prefer to:
options: Hold no relevant cards at all
Hold cards in villain's folding range
Hold the exact cards villain needs to fold
Hold cards in villain's continuing range and not hold cards in their folding range

explanations.A: Ignoring removal entirely forfeits a key bluff-selection edge.
explanations.B: Holding cards in their folding range means you remove hands that would have folded — bad for a bluff.
explanations.C: Blocking the hands that fold reduces the number of folds you get.
explanations.D: Correct. Blocking value/continuing combos lowers the chance of a call, while not blocking folding hands keeps those folds available.

questions.question: A combo draw of a flush draw plus an open-ended straight draw has about 15 outs. Its rough equity by the river is:
options: About 54%
About 45%
About 32%
About 70%

explanations.A: Correct. Rule of 4 gives 15 × 4 = 60% as a rough cap, with the true figure near 54% by the river.
explanations.B: 45% is closer to a 12-out draw.
explanations.C: 32% is closer to a plain open-ender, not a 15-out monster.
explanations.D: 70% overstates even a 15-out combo draw's equity.

questions.question: Semi-bluffing a draw rather than check-calling improves equity realization mainly because:
options: It always completes your draw faster
Folds let you realize the pot immediately, and betting can deny the opponent free improvement
It removes the rake
It forces the dealer to give better cards

explanations.A: Betting does not change the speed or odds of hitting a draw.
explanations.B: Correct. Aggression adds fold equity and can charge or fold out hands that would otherwise outdraw or outlast you.
explanations.C: Betting does not eliminate rake.
explanations.D: Card distribution is random and unaffected by your action.

questions.question: A pure bluff differs from a value bet because the bluff wants the opponent to ___ while the value bet wants the opponent to ___.
options: fold; fold
call; fold
fold; call
call; call

explanations.A: A value bet wants calls, not folds.
explanations.B: This reverses the goals of each bet type.
explanations.C: Correct. Bluffs profit from folds; value bets profit from calls by worse hands.
explanations.D: A bluff wants folds, not calls.

questions.question: Which is NOT a semi-bluff?
options: Raising a flop with a flush draw and two overcards
Betting the turn with an open-ended straight draw
Raising the flop with a gutshot plus backdoor flush draw
Betting the river with a busted draw and no equity

explanations.A: A flush draw with overcards is a classic semi-bluff with significant equity.
explanations.B: Betting an open-ender has both fold equity and draw equity — a semi-bluff.
explanations.C: A gutshot plus backdoor flush adds equity, qualifying as a (light) semi-bluff.
explanations.D: Correct. On the river with a busted draw and no equity, there is no draw left, so it is a pure bluff, not a semi-bluff.

questions.question: Adding fold equity to a draw's pot equity means the total break-even bar for a semi-bluff bet is:
options: Lower than for a pure bluff of the same size
Higher than for a pure bluff of the same size
Exactly the same as a pure bluff
Always 50% regardless of equity

explanations.A: Correct. The draw's equity when called reduces how often villain must fold for the bet to break even.
explanations.B: Backup equity lowers, not raises, the required fold frequency.
explanations.C: Pure bluffs have no backup equity, so their bar is higher.
explanations.D: The threshold depends on bet size and equity, not a fixed 50%.

questions.question: A three-quarter-pot pure bluff needs the opponent to fold approximately:

explanations.A: 33% corresponds to a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: 50% is the pot-sized threshold.
explanations.D: 60% corresponds to a 1.5x-pot bet.

questions.question: Against a pot-sized bet, the defender's minimum defense frequency is:

explanations.A: 33% MDF would correspond to a 2x-pot bet.
explanations.B: 67% MDF corresponds to a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: Correct. MDF = pot/(pot+bet) = 1/(1+1) = 50%.
explanations.D: 75% MDF corresponds to a one-third-pot bet.

questions.question: MDF is an EXPLOITATIVE defense concept that can be ignored when:
options: It is always mandatory regardless of opponent
The bettor is perfectly balanced
You are forced to defend by the rules
The bettor under-bluffs and almost never has air

explanations.A: MDF is a guideline, not a mandate; exploit imbalanced opponents instead.
explanations.B: Against a balanced bettor, defending near MDF is appropriate.
explanations.C: There is no rule forcing defense; it is a strategic choice.
explanations.D: Correct. If a player rarely bluffs, you can over-fold below MDF because their bets are mostly value.

questions.question: As your bet size increases on the river, the share of bluffs in a balanced betting range should:
options: Increase
Decrease
Stay the same
Drop to zero

explanations.A: Correct. Larger bets give the caller worse odds, so you can include a higher proportion of bluffs (e.g., overbets allow ~40% bluffs).
explanations.B: Smaller, not larger, bets support fewer bluffs.
explanations.C: The balanced bluff fraction B/(P+2B) changes with bet size.
explanations.D: You never drop bluffs to zero in a balanced polarized range.

questions.question: You bluff a flop and get called by a likely middle-pair-type range. The turn pairs the top card. This card:
options: Always gives the caller trips
Mostly helps your representable range and pressures the caller's pairs
Is a blank that changes nothing
Forces you to check

explanations.A: The caller only rarely holds the exact card to make trips.
explanations.B: Correct. A top-card pair is a card you can represent (you could hold trips), and it threatens the caller's one-pair hands.
explanations.C: Pairing the top card is a meaningful range-shifting card, not a blank.
explanations.D: Nothing forces a check; barreling is often correct here.

questions.question: Which line tells the LEAST credible bluff story for a big river bet?
options: Raise flop, bet turn, bet river representing a set or straight
Bet flop, bet turn, bet river on a coordinated runout
Check flop, check turn, then suddenly overbet a blank river
Call flop, bet turn after a scare card, bet river

explanations.A: Raise-bet-bet credibly represents strong made hands.
explanations.B: A consistent three-street bet line on a coordinated board tells a coherent story.
explanations.C: Correct. Two checks then a sudden overbet on a blank rarely matches a value line, so observant opponents call more.
explanations.D: Calling then leading after a scare card can credibly represent improvement.

questions.question: Blockers improve a bluff by:
options: Improving your own hand strength to the nuts
Increasing the size of the pot automatically
Guaranteeing the opponent folds
Reducing the number of strong-hand combinations the opponent can hold

explanations.A: A blocker is a single card and does not make your hand the nuts.
explanations.B: Blockers do not change pot size.
explanations.C: Blockers raise fold frequency but never guarantee a fold.
explanations.D: Correct. By removing key cards, blockers shrink villain's continuing combos and raise the chance they fold.

questions.question: On the turn you hold a bare flush draw (9 outs) and decide to semi-bluff shove. The shove has fold equity PLUS roughly what one-card equity if called?
options: About 19%
About 9%
About 35%
About 50%

explanations.A: Correct. Rule of 2: 9 × 2 ≈ 18-19% to hit on the river (one card).
explanations.B: 9% is far too low for a nine-out draw on a single card.
explanations.C: 35% is the two-card (flop-to-river) figure, but only one card remains after the turn.
explanations.D: 50% massively overstates a one-card flush draw.

questions.question: Against a tight, fit-or-fold opponent, the best general adjustment is to:
options: Bluff less and only value bet
Bluff more often, since they fold too much
Never bet at all
Slowplay every strong hand

explanations.A: Cutting bluffs against an over-folder wastes their biggest leak.
explanations.B: Correct. Players who fold too often hand you high fold equity, so increase bluff frequency.
explanations.C: Never betting forfeits both fold equity and value.
explanations.D: Slowplaying everything fails to pressure an opponent who folds readily.

questions.question: When bluffing a draw on the flop, a reason to choose a LARGER size is:
options: Because larger bets reduce your draw's equity
To guarantee you see the river for free
To maximize fold equity and charge worse draws/hands when you do get folds
To make the opponent's call always correct

explanations.A: Bet size does not change your draw's equity.
explanations.B: Betting does not give you a free card.
explanations.C: Correct. A bigger semi-bluff applies more pressure and folds out more equity, though it risks more chips.
explanations.D: Larger bets give the caller worse odds, not automatically correct calls.

questions.question: A bluffer bets $30 into a $60 pot. The break-even fold frequency equals:

explanations.A: 25% would be a one-third-pot bet ($20 into $60).
explanations.B: 50% is the pot-sized threshold.
explanations.C: 40% corresponds to a two-thirds-pot bet.

questions.question: A turn card that is a great scare card for the in-position bettor on K♠ 8♦ 3♣ is:
options: An ace, which you can represent and which beats the caller's kings
A 3, pairing the bottom of the board
An 8, pairing middle and aiding the caller
A 2, a meaningless blank

explanations.A: Correct. The ace adds top pair to your range and threatens the caller's weaker kings and pairs, generating fold equity.
explanations.B: Pairing the bottom 3 changes little and rarely scares the caller.
explanations.C: An 8 helps the caller's middle pairs more than your bluff story.
explanations.D: A blank deuce adds no fold equity beyond the flop.

questions.question: In a four-way pot, the probability that all three opponents fold to your bluff, even if each folds 60% independently, is roughly:
options: About 22%
About 36%
About 80%

explanations.A: 60% is the chance a single opponent folds, not all three.
explanations.B: Correct. 0.6 × 0.6 × 0.6 ≈ 0.216 ≈ 22%, illustrating how multiway crushes fold equity.
explanations.C: 36% would be two opponents folding (0.6 × 0.6).
explanations.D: 80% overstates it; combining three folds lowers the joint probability sharply.

questions.question: A delayed c-bet bluff (checking flop in position, betting turn after villain checks again) gains value because:
options: Checking the flop is always mandatory
It guarantees villain has the nuts
Villain's two checks suggest weakness, raising your fold equity
It removes your positional advantage

explanations.A: Checking the flop is a choice, not a requirement.
explanations.B: Checks usually indicate weakness, not the nuts.
explanations.C: Correct. Two checks cap and weaken villain's range, so a turn stab finds plenty of folds.
explanations.D: Acting last preserves, not removes, your positional edge.

questions.question: Two overcards on the flop (e.g., A-K on a low board) represent roughly how many outs to make top pair, and what two-card equity?
options: 12 outs, about 45%
3 outs, about 12%
9 outs, about 35%
6 outs, about 24%

explanations.A: 12 outs is closer to a combo draw, far more than two overcards.
explanations.B: 3 outs would be a single overcard, not two.
explanations.C: 9 outs and 35% describe a flush draw, not bare overcards.
explanations.D: Correct. Two overcards have 6 outs (3 each), giving about 6 × 4 = 24% by the river.

questions.question: Why does a polarized range justify a large bet size?
options: Big bets pressure bluff-catchers while your value hands still get paid
Big bets only make sense with medium hands
Polarized ranges contain no bluffs
Large bets reduce your value hands' winnings

explanations.A: Correct. With nutted hands and bluffs, big bets maximize value and fold equity, putting bluff-catchers to a tough decision.
explanations.B: Medium hands prefer small bets or checks, not large ones.
explanations.C: Polarized ranges are defined by including bluffs alongside value.
explanations.D: Large bets increase, not reduce, the winnings from value hands that get called.

questions.question: You triple-barrel bluff and villain tank-calls the river. Going forward, the lesson is to:
options: Bluff them even bigger next time
Recognize this villain calls down light and reduce future bluffs against them
Never value bet them again
Assume all opponents play identically

explanations.A: Escalating bluffs against a proven caller compounds losses.
explanations.B: Correct. A light call-down signals low fold equity, so dial back bluffs and value bet them more.
explanations.C: You should value bet a caller more, not stop entirely.
explanations.D: Opponents differ; profiling each one is the point of adjusting.

questions.question: On a 7♥ 6♥ 2♣ 9♥ 4♠ board, which hand best blocks villain's nut flush for a river bluff?
options: T♣ 8♦ (busted straight)
A♠ K♠
A♥ x (any ace of hearts)
K♦ Q♦

explanations.A: A busted straight with no heart blocks nothing relevant.
explanations.B: A♠ K♠ holds no heart and blocks none of the flush combos.
explanations.C: Correct. The A♥ blocks the nut heart flush, the strongest hand villain can call with, increasing fold equity.
explanations.D: K♦ Q♦ holds no heart, so it blocks no flush combos.

questions.question: A semi-bluff's expected value can be written as fold% × (pot won) plus called% × (equity-based EV when called). The second term is what distinguishes it from a pure bluff because for a pure bluff it is:
options: Equal to the pot
Always larger than the first term
Always positive and large
Approximately zero (or negative) since there is no backup equity

explanations.A: Pure bluffs do not win the pot when called, so it is not equal to the pot.
explanations.B: For a pure bluff the called branch is a loss, not larger than the fold branch.
explanations.C: Pure bluffs lose when called; the term is not positive.
explanations.D: Correct. A pure bluff has negligible equity when called, so the called-branch EV is near zero or negative.

questions.question: Why does a draw-heavy turn often REDUCE your fold equity when continuing a bluff?
options: The opponent has many drawing hands that can profitably continue
Draws are illegal to call with
Wet boards always fold out everyone
Draw-heavy boards have no made hands

explanations.A: Correct. Coordinated cards give villain straight/flush draws and made hands that call, lowering fold equity.
explanations.B: Players routinely and legally call with draws.
explanations.C: Wet boards generate continues, not mass folds.
explanations.D: Wet boards have plenty of made hands plus draws.

questions.question: If a half-pot bluff needs villain to fold 33% to break even and villain actually folds 50%, the bluff is:
options: Break-even exactly
Profitable, since actual folds exceed the break-even point
Unprofitable
Impossible to evaluate

explanations.A: Break-even is at 33%; 50% is above it.
explanations.B: Correct. Folding more than the 33% break-even makes the bluff +EV.
explanations.C: Exceeding the break-even fold rate is profitable, not losing.
explanations.D: With the numbers given the EV is clearly positive.

questions.question: When river bluffing, holding a card that is in villain's FOLDING range is bad because it:
options: Always makes villain call
Increases the pot
Removes a hand that would have folded, shrinking your fold equity
Improves your hand to a flush

explanations.A: It reduces folds, but does not force a call every time.
explanations.B: Card removal does not change the pot size.
explanations.C: Correct. Holding cards villain would fold means fewer of those folding combos remain in their range, reducing folds.
explanations.D: A single removed card does not make you a flush.

questions.question: An overbet bluff on a brick river is most credible when the runout:
options: Makes your range entirely bluffs
Has put four to a flush on a board you cannot have
Clearly favors the caller's range
Favors your range and you can hold the nutted hands you represent

explanations.A: An all-bluff overbetting range is exploitable by always calling.
explanations.B: If you cannot credibly have the flush, the overbet story collapses.
explanations.C: A caller-favoring runout undercuts your overbet representation.
explanations.D: Correct. Overbets require nut advantage so your representation is believable and bluff-catchers fear the worst.

questions.question: Good hands to check-raise bluff on a flush-draw flop are typically:
options: Draws and backdoor combos that block value and improve when called
Made full houses
The absolute nuts only
Pure air with no equity and no blockers

explanations.A: Correct. Semi-bluff check-raises with draws block value, add equity, and apply pressure — the ideal mix.
explanations.B: Full houses prefer to keep villain's range in, not blast them out with a raise bluff.
explanations.C: Raising only the nuts is value, not a check-raise bluff.
explanations.D: Pure air with no equity is the weakest, least efficient check-raise bluff.

questions.question: The biggest risk of habitually bluffing too much is that:
options: You will always win the pot
Observant opponents start calling you down lighter, collapsing your fold equity
Your value bets get paid more
Nothing — bluffing more is always better

explanations.A: Over-bluffing does not guarantee wins; it invites calls.
explanations.B: Correct. Over-bluffing makes your bets less believable, so opponents call more and your bluffs fail.
explanations.C: Over-bluffing can actually get value paid more, but the dominant cost is being called down — the leak is over-bluffing.
explanations.D: There is a balance; bluffing too much is exploitable.

questions.question: Approximately what is the river-card (one-card) equity of an 8-out open-ended straight draw on the turn?
options: About 8%
About 31%
About 17%
About 50%

explanations.A: 8% is closer to a four-out gutshot on one card.
explanations.B: 31% is the two-card equity, but only one card remains after the turn.
explanations.C: Correct. Rule of 2: 8 × 2 ≈ 16-17% to complete on the river.
explanations.D: 50% massively overstates a one-card open-ender.

questions.question: Why does the preflop raiser usually have a range (and nut) advantage on an A-K-x flop?
options: Range advantage never applies on ace-high boards
The caller always folds aces
High cards favor the blinds' range
Their raising range contains more strong aces and big broadways than the caller's

explanations.A: Range advantage applies strongly precisely on high-card boards.
explanations.B: The caller does not categorically fold aces.
explanations.C: High broadway cards favor the raiser's range, not the blinds'.
explanations.D: Correct. Raisers hold AA, AK, AQ, KK more often, so high boards favor them and support aggressive bluffing.

questions.question: Which is the better semi-bluff RAISE on the flop, all else equal?
options: A naked gutshot with a backdoor flush draw and two overcards
Bottom pair with no draw
A made set
Ace-high with no draw

explanations.A: Correct. The gutshot plus backdoor flush plus overcards has multiple equity sources, making it a strong semi-bluff candidate.
explanations.B: Bottom pair has weak equity and little upside as a raise bluff.
explanations.C: A set is a value raise, not a semi-bluff.
explanations.D: Ace-high with no draw lacks the backup equity that defines a good semi-bluff.

questions.question: If your bluff has zero fold equity AND zero equity when called, its EV is:
options: Positive
Negative — you simply lose the chips you bet
Zero
Equal to the pot

explanations.A: There is no path to profit without folds or equity.
explanations.B: Correct. With no folds and no way to win at showdown, betting only loses the wagered chips.
explanations.C: You lose chips, so EV is negative, not zero.
explanations.D: You do not win the pot; you forfeit your bet.

questions.question: A 1.5x-pot overbet pure bluff needs the opponent to fold at least:

explanations.A: 50% corresponds to a pot-sized bet.
explanations.B: 67% corresponds to a 2x-pot bet.
explanations.D: 75% corresponds to a 3x-pot bet.

questions.question: Against a two-thirds-pot bet, the defender's MDF is closest to:

explanations.A: 50% MDF is for a pot-sized bet.
explanations.B: 57% MDF corresponds to a three-quarter-pot bet.
explanations.C: 67% MDF corresponds to a half-pot bet.

questions.question: For a balanced 2x-pot overbet on the river, the bluff fraction of the betting range is closest to:

explanations.A: Correct. Bluffs = B/(P+2B) = 2/(1+4) = 2/5 = 40% for a 2x-pot bet.
explanations.B: 33% is the pot-sized bluff fraction.
explanations.C: 25% is the half-pot bluff fraction.
explanations.D: 50% would over-bluff even an overbet.

questions.question: You face an opponent whose line caps them at one pair (they checked back a draw-heavy flop). The best exploit on a scary turn is to:
options: Check and give up
Bet large/overbet as a bluff, since they cannot have the strongest hands
Only bet your own one-pair hands
Fold all your bluffs

explanations.A: Checking forfeits the exploit against a capped, foldable range.
explanations.B: Correct. A capped range struggles versus big bets, so polarized large bluffs apply maximum pressure.
explanations.C: Betting only one pair misses the chance to leverage your nut advantage with bluffs.
explanations.D: Folding bluffs against a capped opponent wastes their vulnerability.

questions.question: A second-barrel bluff is generally WEAKER when the turn:
options: Completes a draw you have been representing
Brings a scare card you can represent
Is a blank that does not change either range
Pairs the top card

explanations.A: A completing card lets you credibly represent the made hand.
explanations.B: A scare card you can represent boosts fold equity for a barrel.
explanations.C: Correct. A blank shifts no ranges and adds no new fold equity, so the caller continues at the same rate.
explanations.D: Pairing the top card is a representable, range-shifting card that aids barreling.

questions.question: Sizing tells in a bluff story matter because:
options: Only the river size matters
Bigger is always more believable
Sizing has no information
Sizes that mismatch your value bets in similar spots can expose the bluff

explanations.A: Sizes across all streets contribute to the story, not just the river.
explanations.B: Bigger is not automatically believable; it must match a credible value size.
explanations.C: Sizing carries significant information about your range.
explanations.D: Correct. If you size bluffs differently from value in the same situation, observant opponents detect it and adjust.

questions.question: When bluffing to make villain fold a straight, the ideal blocker is a card that:
options: Reduces the combos of the straight villain can hold
Pairs your own kicker
Is a low card unrelated to the straight
Is in your opponent's folding range

explanations.A: Correct. Holding a key card of the straight removes those combos, so villain less often has the hand and folds more.
explanations.B: Pairing your kicker does not block villain's straight.
explanations.C: An unrelated low card blocks none of the target straights.
explanations.D: Blocking folding hands is counterproductive — you want to block calls.

questions.question: A turn semi-bluff shove with a flush draw is +EV when:
options: Only when villain always folds
The combination of fold equity and draw equity exceeds the risk of the shove
Only when the flush is already complete
Never, because shoving draws is always bad

explanations.A: You do not need villain to always fold; equity when called lowers the requirement.
explanations.B: Correct. The two ways to win — folds now plus completing when called — together can clear the break-even bar.
explanations.C: If the flush were complete it would be a value shove, not a semi-bluff.
explanations.D: Semi-bluff shoves are frequently profitable, not always bad.

questions.question: Which texture most favors a high c-bet bluff frequency for the preflop raiser?
options: 7♠ 7♥ 6♦ (paired and connected)
T♦ 9♦ 8♣ (very wet)
A♥ 6♣ 2♦ (dry, ace-high)
5♥ 4♥ 3♥ (monotone, connected)

explanations.A: A paired connected board gives the caller trips and straight draws to defend.
explanations.B: A wet T-9-8 hits the caller's range and offers many draws to continue.
explanations.C: Correct. A dry ace-high board favors the raiser's range and gives the caller few continues, supporting frequent small c-bet bluffs.
explanations.D: A monotone connected board is rich in made hands and draws.

questions.question: Stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) affects fold equity because:
options: Fold equity only exists at SPR exactly 1
SPR has no effect on folds
A high SPR means opponents never fold
A low SPR means smaller remaining bets generate less fold equity since opponents are pot-committed

explanations.A: Fold equity exists across a wide range of SPRs, not a single value.
explanations.B: SPR strongly influences commitment and therefore fold frequency.
explanations.C: High SPR generally allows MORE fold equity, not none.
explanations.D: Correct. With a low SPR, opponents are often committed and fold less, reducing fold equity; high SPR leaves room for meaningful pressure.

questions.question: You bet $100 into a $100 pot and your opponent must fold 50% for your bluff to break even. If they fold only 40%, the bluff is:
options: Unprofitable, since 40% is below the 50% break-even
Profitable
Break-even
Impossible to evaluate

explanations.A: Correct. Folding less than the break-even threshold makes the pure bluff lose money on average.
explanations.B: Below the break-even fold rate the bluff is -EV.
explanations.C: Break-even is at 50%; 40% is below it.
explanations.D: The numbers clearly show a negative EV.

questions.question: A flush draw plus a gutshot gives roughly 12 outs. Its approximate two-card (flop-to-river) equity is:
options: About 24%
About 45%
About 32%
About 60%

explanations.A: 24% is closer to two overcards (6 outs).
explanations.B: Correct. Rule of 4: 12 × 4 = 48%, with the true figure near 45% by the river.
explanations.C: 32% is closer to a plain open-ender (8 outs).
explanations.D: 60% overstates a 12-out draw; that is closer to a 15-out monster.

questions.question: You bluff the flop and turn; the river is a brick and villain has called twice and looks committed. With pure air, the best play is usually to:
options: Min-bet to 'block'
Always blast a big third barrel
Give up and check/fold rather than fire a third barrel into a sticky range
Open-fold before villain acts

explanations.A: A block bet still risks chips with little fold equity and weak representation here.
explanations.B: Firing into a committed caller with no fold equity is -EV.
explanations.C: Correct. Two calls into a brick river signal low fold equity; an extra bluff barrel mostly burns chips.
explanations.D: You cannot fold out of turn when it is your action to check or bet.

questions.question: On Q♠ J♠ 4♦ 8♠ 2♥, you want to bluff representing the nut flush. Which blocker is most powerful?
options: 4♣ in your hand
K♥ in your hand
Q♦ in your hand
A♠ in your hand

explanations.A: 4♣ blocks a low pair, irrelevant to the flush you represent.
explanations.B: K♥ blocks no spade flush; wrong suit.
explanations.C: Q♦ blocks a top pair, not the nut flush.
explanations.D: Correct. The A♠ blocks the nut flush combos, the very hands that would call, boosting fold equity.

questions.question: A 'maniac' who raises and bets relentlessly is best countered by:
options: Bluffing them less and inducing/value betting, since they bluff themselves
Out-bluffing them at every turn
Folding every hand
Only checking the nuts

explanations.A: Correct. A maniac supplies the aggression; you profit by trapping and value betting rather than out-bluffing them.
explanations.B: Trying to out-bluff a maniac leads to wild variance and spew.
explanations.C: Folding everything surrenders the value their over-aggression offers.
explanations.D: Checking only the nuts forfeits value from their constant bluffing.

questions.question: The main downside of semi-bluffing very aggressively with weak draws is:
options: You can never win the pot
You can get raised off your equity by stronger hands
Draws are illegal to bet
It always reduces variance

explanations.A: Semi-bluffs can win by folds or by hitting; they certainly can win.
explanations.B: Correct. Aggressive semi-bluffs risk facing a raise that forces you to fold your draw's equity.
explanations.C: Betting draws is legal and standard.
explanations.D: Aggressive semi-bluffing typically increases variance, not reduces it.

questions.question: Using the SAME size for value bets and bluffs in a given spot is important because:
options: It is required by poker rules
It guarantees more folds
It prevents opponents from reading your hand strength from your sizing
It lowers the pot every time

explanations.A: Sizing is a strategic choice, not a rule.
explanations.B: Matching sizes does not by itself create more folds.
explanations.C: Correct. Consistent sizing keeps your range balanced and disguised, so opponents cannot exploit a 'big = value, small = bluff' tell.
explanations.D: Bet size affects the pot, but consistency does not lower it.

questions.question: Against a quarter-pot bet, a defender's MDF is approximately:

explanations.A: 50% MDF is for a pot-sized bet.
explanations.B: 67% MDF is for a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: 75% MDF is for a one-third-pot bet.
explanations.D: Correct. MDF = 1/(1+0.25) = 1/1.25 = 80% against a quarter-pot bet.

questions.question: Which best captures why a pure bluff and a thin value bet are NOT the same?
options: A bluff profits only when better hands fold; thin value profits when worse hands call
They both want folds
They both want calls
They are identical in every way

explanations.A: Correct. The win conditions are opposite: bluffs want folds from better, thin value wants calls from worse.
explanations.B: Thin value wants calls, not folds.
explanations.C: A pure bluff wants folds, not calls.
explanations.D: Their goals are opposite, so they are not identical.

questions.question: Increasing your bet size raises fold equity only up to a point because:
options: Bigger bets always fold out everyone
Eventually opponents only continue with hands that beat you, so extra size just risks more
Fold equity grows without limit
Size has no relationship to folds

explanations.A: Even huge bets get called by the top of villain's range.
explanations.B: Correct. Past a threshold, only strong hands call, so larger bluffs risk more chips without proportionally more folds.
explanations.C: Fold equity is bounded; you cannot fold out hands that beat you indefinitely.
explanations.D: Size does affect folds, just with diminishing returns.

questions.question: A profitable double-barrel bluff plan is best set up by:
options: Betting flop with hands that can never barrel any turn
Only c-betting the nuts
C-betting flop with hands and bluffs that can credibly continue on many turns
Checking the flop with the whole range

explanations.A: Bluffs that cannot continue on any turn waste flop fold equity and must give up.
explanations.B: C-betting only the nuts is unbalanced and forfeits fold equity from bluffs.
explanations.C: Correct. Choosing flop bluffs with backdoor equity and good barreling cards keeps fold equity alive on later streets.
explanations.D: Checking the entire range surrenders flop initiative and fold equity.

questions.question: On the flop with a set, you have about 10 outs to a full house or quads by the river. This is roughly:
options: About 75%
Exactly 50%
About 5%
About 22-34% to improve

explanations.A: 75% wildly overstates the chance to fill up.
explanations.B: 50% overstates the improvement odds for a set.
explanations.C: 5% is far too low for a flopped set's improvement to a full house.
explanations.D: Correct. Around 10 outs by the river gives roughly 33% by rule of 4 (the precise boat/quads figure for a flopped set is about 33% by the river).

questions.question: If you are betting a half-pot river with 6 value combos in your range, how many bluff combos keep you balanced (3:1)?
options: 2 bluff combos
1 bluff combo
3 bluff combos
6 bluff combos

explanations.A: Correct. A 3:1 value-to-bluff ratio with 6 value combos calls for 2 bluff combos.
explanations.B: 1 bluff would be a 6:1 ratio — under-bluffed for a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: 3 bluffs would make it 2:1, the pot-sized ratio, over-bluffing a half-pot bet.
explanations.D: 6 bluffs would be 1:1, heavily over-bluffed.

questions.question: On a turn that brings a third flush card, the player who can MOST credibly bluff is the one who:
options: Has shown weakness by checking twice
Has been the aggressor and can represent the flush given their range
Holds the lowest card of the flush suit only
Cannot have any flush in their range

explanations.A: A player who showed weakness has a capped, less believable range.
explanations.B: Correct. The aggressor whose range includes suited combos can represent the completed flush, gaining fold equity.
explanations.C: A single low flush card does not let you represent the made flush convincingly.
explanations.D: If your range cannot contain a flush, the flush-card bluff is not credible.

questions.question: Holding a card that blocks villain's value combos but NOT their bluff-catchers is ideal because it:
options: Improves your hand to a straight
Guarantees a fold every time
Reduces the hands that would call without removing hands that would fold
Makes the pot bigger

explanations.A: A single blocker card does not make your hand a straight.
explanations.B: Blockers raise fold frequency but never guarantee folds.
explanations.C: Correct. You want to remove calls and preserve folds, which exactly this blocker pattern accomplishes.
explanations.D: Card removal does not change pot size.

questions.question: In a multiway pot, a good adjustment for your bluffing range is to:
options: Treat it exactly like a heads-up pot
Bluff every hand because more dead money is in the pot
Always overbet as a bluff
Bluff much less and only with high-equity semi-bluffs

explanations.A: Multiway dynamics differ sharply from heads-up and require fewer bluffs.
explanations.B: More opponents means lower fold equity, so bluffing every hand loses.
explanations.C: Overbet bluffing into multiple players is usually high-risk and low-reward.
explanations.D: Correct. With more players, pure bluffs rarely get through, so favor strong semi-bluffs and tighten bluffing overall.

questions.question: Your line is: 3-bet preflop, c-bet flop, check turn, bet river big. On a dry board, the river bet is hardest to believe as value if:
options: Your turn check usually means giving up, so your range is capped
You can credibly hold many slowplayed monsters after checking turn
The board pairs and you can represent a full house
You have shown consistent aggression

explanations.A: Correct. If checking the turn typically caps your range, a sudden big river bet looks more like a bluff and gets called.
explanations.B: If you DO have many slowplays, the bet is more believable, not less — the opposite of the question.
explanations.C: A pairing board that lets you represent a boat makes the bet MORE credible.
explanations.D: Consistent aggression strengthens, not weakens, a value story.

questions.question: A min-bet bluff (very small relative to pot) is risky as a pure bluff because:
options: It needs the opponent to fold over 90%
Although the break-even fold % is low, opponents get great odds and rarely fold enough
Small bets are illegal
It always wins the pot

explanations.A: A min-bet's break-even fold rate is low, not above 90%.
explanations.B: Correct. A tiny bet has a low break-even fold rate, but it offers the opponent such good pot odds that they call very wide.
explanations.C: Small bets are legal within table minimums.
explanations.D: Small bluffs do not guarantee winning; they often get called.

questions.question: All else equal, a semi-bluff with MORE outs is better than one with fewer outs because:
options: More outs reduce your fold equity
More outs guarantee a fold
The extra equity when called raises EV and lowers the required fold frequency
Outs do not affect semi-bluff EV

explanations.A: More outs do not reduce fold equity; they add a second way to win.
explanations.B: Outs improve the called branch but do not force folds.
explanations.C: Correct. More outs mean higher equity when called, so the bet profits at a lower fold rate.
explanations.D: Outs directly increase the called-branch equity and thus EV.

questions.question: Why does being out of position make triple-barrel bluffing harder?
options: Position is irrelevant to multi-street bluffs
You can never bet out of position
Out of position you always have the nuts
You must commit chips before seeing villain's response, and they can float and take pots away

explanations.A: Position significantly affects multi-street bluff success.
explanations.B: You can bet OOP; it is simply less effective.
explanations.C: Being OOP says nothing about your hand strength.
explanations.D: Correct. Acting first means you bet blind to villain's reaction, and in-position floats can punish your barrels.

questions.question: Why is bluffing a low, connected flop (e.g., 6♣ 5♦ 4♠) often bad for the preflop raiser heads-up vs a big-blind caller?
options: The board hits the caller's range of small/connected hands harder than the raiser's
The raiser always flops the nuts there
Low boards have no draws
The caller always folds low boards

explanations.A: Correct. Big-blind defending ranges contain many small connectors that smash 6-5-4, so c-bet bluffs face more continues.
explanations.B: The raiser rarely has the nuts on a low connected board.
explanations.C: Low connected boards are loaded with straight and pair-plus-draw combos.
explanations.D: The caller defends low connected boards widely, not folding readily.

questions.question: Equity denial via a semi-bluff bet means:
options: Giving the opponent a free card
Folding out hands that had outs to beat you, capturing the pot before they realize equity
Reducing your own equity to zero
Guaranteeing you hit your draw

explanations.A: Betting charges the opponent rather than giving a free card.
explanations.B: Correct. Betting denies opponents the chance to outdraw you for free, an important benefit of semi-bluffing.
explanations.C: Your equity is unchanged by betting.
explanations.D: Betting does not change whether your draw completes.

questions.question: On the river you bet pot with 8 value combos. To stay balanced at 2:1, how many bluff combos should you have?

explanations.A: 2 bluffs would be a 4:1 ratio, under-bluffing a pot-sized bet.
explanations.B: 3 bluffs is closer to 8:3, still slightly under-bluffed for pot.
explanations.C: Correct. 2:1 with 8 value combos calls for 4 bluff combos.
explanations.D: 8 bluffs would be 1:1, heavily over-bluffed for a pot-sized bet.

questions.question: A common reason an overbet generates extra fold equity is that:
options: Overbets are never called
It guarantees the opponent is drawing dead
It reduces the opponent's pot odds to call below zero
It puts maximum pressure on bluff-catchers, who must risk many chips to call

explanations.A: Overbets do get called by strong hands; they are not uncallable.
explanations.B: An overbet says nothing about the opponent being drawing dead.
explanations.C: Pot odds cannot be below zero; the overbet simply makes them worse for the caller.
explanations.D: Correct. The large size threatens the opponent's stack, making marginal bluff-catchers fold more often.

questions.question: A disciplined bluffer treats chips already bet on earlier streets as:
options: Sunk cost, irrelevant to whether the next bluff is +EV
A reason to keep bluffing to recover them
A guarantee the bluff will work
Money that must always be protected with another barrel

explanations.A: Correct. Past bets are sunk; each new decision should be judged on its own forward-looking EV.
explanations.B: Chasing sunk costs leads to spewy, losing bluffs.
explanations.C: Prior bets do not make a future bluff succeed.
explanations.D: Protecting sunk chips with more bluffs is a classic fallacy.

questions.question: You want to bluff-raise the river representing a straight on 9♠ 8♦ 5♣ 7♥ 2♣. Which holding has the best straight blocker?
options: A hand containing a 2
A hand containing a T or a 6 (blocks the nut straights)
A hand containing a 9 only
A hand containing two clubs

explanations.A: A 2 blocks only a low pair, irrelevant to the straights.
explanations.B: Correct. A T or 6 blocks the J-T / 6-x straight combos, reducing the strong straights villain can hold.
explanations.C: A single 9 blocks a pair of nines, not the key straights.
explanations.D: Two clubs is relevant to a flush, not the straight you are representing.

questions.question: Against a strong, balanced regular, your bluffing should be:
options: Eliminated entirely
Heavily over-weighted because regs always fold
Reasonably balanced, since exploitative over-bluffing will be punished
Random with no thought to ratios

explanations.A: Removing all bluffs makes your value bets easy to fold to.
explanations.B: Strong regs do not always fold; over-bluffing them gets snapped off.
explanations.C: Correct. A balanced opponent cannot be easily exploited, so balanced bluffing protects you from counter-exploitation.
explanations.D: Random bluffing ignores balance and is exploitable.

questions.question: A backdoor flush draw with two overcards on the flop is best categorized as:
options: A made flush
A pure bluff with zero equity
A value hand
A light semi-bluff with modest backup equity and good barreling potential

explanations.A: A backdoor draw is not a completed flush.
explanations.B: It has real, if modest, equity, so it is not a pure bluff.
explanations.C: It is not a made hand, so it is not value yet.
explanations.D: Correct. Backdoor equity plus overcards gives some way to improve and many good turn barrel cards, making it a light semi-bluff.

questions.question: Two bluffs of the same size differ in fold equity mainly due to:
options: The opponent's range and tendencies, plus the board and your representable hands
The color of the chips
The time of day
Nothing — size alone determines fold equity

explanations.A: Correct. Fold equity depends on who you face, their range, the texture, and how credibly you represent value — not just size.
explanations.B: Chip color is irrelevant to fold equity.
explanations.C: Time of day does not affect the math of fold equity.
explanations.D: Size matters, but range, board, and story also drive fold equity.

questions.question: A pure bluff that risks 1 unit to win a 4-unit pot (quarter-pot bet) breaks even at what fold frequency?

explanations.A: 25% corresponds to a one-third-pot bet.
explanations.B: Correct. Break-even = bet/(bet+pot) = 1/(1+4) = 1/5 = 20%.
explanations.C: 33% corresponds to a half-pot bet.
explanations.D: 50% corresponds to a pot-sized bet.

questions.question: Why does MDF DECREASE as the bettor's size increases?
options: MDF actually increases with bet size
Larger bets mean the defender must call everything
Larger bets give better immediate odds to the bettor, so the defender can fold more before bluffs become free
MDF is constant regardless of size

explanations.A: MDF decreases, not increases, with larger sizes.
explanations.B: Bigger bets allow MORE folding, not mandatory calling.
explanations.C: Correct. MDF = pot/(pot+bet) falls as bet grows, so against bigger bets you may fold more of your range.
explanations.D: MDF clearly varies with bet size.

questions.question: A pair hoping to improve to a set has 2 outs. Its rough equity to hit by the river from the flop is:
options: About 35%
About 16%
About 25%
About 8%

explanations.A: 35% is the equity of a nine-out flush draw.
explanations.B: 16% corresponds to a four-out gutshot.
explanations.C: 25% greatly overstates a two-out draw.
explanations.D: Correct. Rule of 4: 2 × 4 = 8% (about 8.4%) to make a set by the river.

questions.question: After c-betting K-7-2 rainbow and getting called, the WORST turn card to barrel as a bluff is usually:
options: A blank like the 3, which changes nothing for either range
An ace, which you can represent
A queen, which adds equity and pressures kings
A jack, a clean overcard you can rep

explanations.A: Correct. A low blank does not shift ranges or add fold equity, so it is the least effective barrel card.
explanations.B: An ace is a strong overcard you can represent for fold equity.
explanations.C: A queen is an overcard that pressures the caller's weaker kings.
explanations.D: A jack is a clean overcard that supports a barrel.

questions.question: Representing a flush when you hold a card of that suit (a blocker) makes your bluff:
options: Less credible, since you cannot have a flush
More credible, since villain is less likely to hold the made flush
Irrelevant to credibility
Always a value bet

explanations.A: Holding one suit card does not preclude representing a flush; it strengthens the bluff.
explanations.B: Correct. Blocking a flush card removes some of villain's made flushes, so your story is harder to call down.
explanations.C: Blocker effects directly influence credibility and fold equity.
explanations.D: Holding a single suited card is not a made flush; it remains a bluff.

questions.question: If you NEVER bluff your big river bets, an observant opponent should:
options: Raise as a bluff every time
Call every time
Fold all bluff-catchers, since your bets are pure value
Do nothing differently

explanations.A: Raise-bluffing into a pure value range is suicidal.
explanations.B: Calling a never-bluff range with bluff-catchers loses to your value.
explanations.C: Correct. Without bluffs, your bets are all value, so bluff-catchers should simply fold.
explanations.D: An exploitable, un-bluffed range demands adjustment — over-folding.

questions.question: A check-raise bluff usually represents MORE strength than a flat call because:
options: Check-raises can only be value
Calling always means the nuts
Raising means you must have air
Raising shows a hand willing to build the pot, narrowing to strong made hands and draws

explanations.A: Check-raises can be value or bluffs in a balanced range.
explanations.B: A flat call usually represents medium strength, not the nuts.
explanations.C: A raise represents strength; it is not a tell for air.
explanations.D: Correct. A raise credibly represents strong hands, generating fold equity that a passive call cannot.

questions.question: A semi-bluff bet of $50 into $100 with a flush draw (about 35% to hit by river) is more attractive than the same bet with 7-2 offsuit air because:
options: The draw adds ~35% equity on the called branch, lowering the needed fold frequency
7-2 offsuit folds out more hands
Air has more equity than a flush draw
The draw reduces your fold equity

explanations.A: Correct. The flush draw wins a meaningful share when called, so it clears break-even at a lower fold rate than pure air.
explanations.B: 7-2 offsuit does not fold out more hands than a flush draw at the same size.
explanations.C: A flush draw has far more equity than 7-2 offsuit air.
explanations.D: The draw does not reduce fold equity; it adds backup equity.

questions.question: A reason to bluff with a SMALL size on a dry board is:
options: Small bets always fold out more than big bets
A small bet achieves a high enough fold rate cheaply because villain's range is weak
Small bets are required on dry boards
Small bets have no fold equity

explanations.A: Big bets generally fold out more; small bets simply risk less.
explanations.B: Correct. On dry boards villain misses often, so a small c-bet bluff folds out enough air at low risk.
explanations.C: Sizing is a choice, not a requirement.
explanations.D: Small bets do generate fold equity, just at lower risk and reward.

questions.question: On a final board with two pair possible, holding a card that pairs the board in your hand:
options: Guarantees you a full house
Has no removal effect
Blocks villain's two-pair and trips combos, aiding a bluff
Makes villain call more

explanations.A: A single paired card is not a full house.
explanations.B: It does have a removal effect on villain's paired holdings.
explanations.C: Correct. Holding a board card reduces the trips/two-pair combos villain can make, improving fold equity.
explanations.D: Reducing villain's strong combos tends to increase folds, not calls.

questions.question: The player WITHOUT range advantage on a given flop should generally:
options: Never fold
Bluff every hand to seize initiative
Always overbet
Bluff less frequently and check more, deferring to the range-advantaged player

explanations.A: Refusing to fold from a weak range bleeds chips.
explanations.B: Bluffing every hand without range support is easily exploited.
explanations.C: Overbetting from a disadvantaged range invites calls and raises.
explanations.D: Correct. Lacking range advantage means fewer strong hands to back bluffs, so a higher check frequency is correct.

questions.question: A bluff into a player who has shown extreme commitment (e.g., shoved most of their stack earlier) has:
options: Very low fold equity, since they are pot-committed
Maximum fold equity
No relationship to commitment
Guaranteed success

explanations.A: Correct. A committed opponent rarely folds, so further bluffs find little fold equity.
explanations.B: Commitment reduces, not maximizes, fold equity.
explanations.C: Commitment level strongly affects fold frequency.
explanations.D: No bluff is guaranteed, least of all against a committed player.

questions.question: You bet two-thirds pot ($40 into $60) as a pure bluff. The break-even fold frequency is:

explanations.A: 33% corresponds to a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: 50% corresponds to a pot-sized bet.
explanations.D: 60% corresponds to a 1.5x-pot bet.

questions.question: Which runout most increases the in-position bettor's bluffing fold equity on the river after they barreled a draw-heavy turn?
options: A card that improves the caller's likely pairs
A blank that bricks all draws
A card completing the obvious draw they represented
A board pair that helps the caller's trips

explanations.A: A card helping the caller's pairs reduces their fold frequency.
explanations.B: A brick fails to complete the story and adds no extra fold equity.
explanations.C: Correct. Completing the represented draw lets the bettor credibly hold the nuts, maximizing fold equity.
explanations.D: A board pair aiding the caller's trips lowers fold equity.

questions.question: Why are medium-strength hands usually bet SMALL or checked rather than used as big bluffs/value?
options: They always belong in a polarized range
They are the strongest hands
They have zero equity
