They want to control the pot and realize showdown value, not get raised off it

explanations.A: Medium hands fit a merged/condensed range, not a polarized one.
explanations.B: Medium hands are not the strongest; that is the polarized value tier.
explanations.C: Medium hands have meaningful showdown equity, not zero.
explanations.D: Correct. Medium hands prefer pot control and showdown rather than bloating the pot where they get blown off equity.

questions.question: Which is a sound trigger to ABANDON a planned multi-street bluff?
options: Villain check-raises or leads into you, representing real strength
Villain checks to you again, showing weakness
A scare card you can represent arrives
Villain visibly looks uncomfortable

explanations.A: Correct. Aggression from villain signals strength and kills fold equity, so giving up is wise.
explanations.B: Another check shows weakness, a reason to CONTINUE the bluff.
explanations.C: A representable scare card is a reason to keep barreling.
explanations.D: Physical tells are unreliable and not a sound primary trigger.

questions.question: A semi-bluff's two ways to win are:
options: Opponent calls now, or the board pairs
Opponent folds now, or you complete your draw later
You always have the best hand, or you fold
The pot shrinks, or the dealer mucks

explanations.A: Wanting calls is value betting; board pairing is not the semi-bluff win path.
explanations.B: Correct. A semi-bluff captures fold equity immediately and retains draw equity if called.
explanations.C: Semi-bluffs rarely already have the best hand; that would be value.
explanations.D: Pot shrinking and dealer mucks are not the ways a semi-bluff wins.

questions.question: A 'nit' who only continues with very strong hands should be bluffed:
options: Only with the nuts
Never, because they call too much
More often on later streets, since they over-fold everything but premiums
Exactly as often as a calling station

explanations.A: Bluffing requires not having the nuts; that would be value.
explanations.B: A nit folds too much, not calls too much.
explanations.C: Correct. A nit folds frequently, so they offer high fold equity and can be bluffed more, especially on big streets.
explanations.D: A nit and a station are opposites; you bluff them very differently.

questions.question: On the turn, a combo draw of a flush draw plus an open-ender (about 15 outs) has roughly what ONE-card equity?
options: About 8%
About 54%
About 19%
About 32%

explanations.A: 8% is far too low for a 15-out draw.
explanations.B: 54% is the two-card (flop-to-river) figure, but only one card remains.
explanations.C: 19% is a single nine-out flush draw on one card, fewer outs than this combo.
explanations.D: Correct. Rule of 2: 15 × 2 = 30%, with the true one-card figure around 32%.

questions.question: A defender who folds MORE than MDF against a balanced bettor is:
options: Over-folding, allowing the bettor's bluffs to profit
Playing perfectly
Under-folding
Immune to bluffs

explanations.A: Correct. Folding above MDF means the bettor can profitably bluff any two cards.
explanations.B: Against a balanced bettor, perfect defense is near MDF, not above it.
explanations.C: Folding more than MDF is over-folding, not under-folding.
explanations.D: Over-folding makes you MORE vulnerable to bluffs, not immune.

questions.question: When choosing river bluffs, you generally prefer hands that BLOCK villain's value and do NOT block their:
options: Strongest possible hands
Folding (bluff-catching) range
Nut combos
Drawing hands that missed

explanations.A: Blocking villain's strongest hands is exactly what you DO want.
explanations.B: Correct. You want to keep villain's folding hands in their range, so you should not hold those cards.
explanations.C: Blocking nut combos is desirable, not something to avoid.
explanations.D: Missed draws that fold are part of the folding range you want to preserve.

questions.question: A board that strongly favors bluffing is one where:
options: The opponent has all the nutted combos
Both ranges are equally strong
Your range is uncapped/strong and the opponent's range is weak and capped
Your range is entirely bluffs

explanations.A: If the opponent owns the nutted hands, bluffing into them is poor.
explanations.B: Equal ranges give no special bluffing edge.
explanations.C: Correct. A nut and range advantage lets your bluffs credibly represent strength against a foldable, capped opponent.
explanations.D: A pure-bluff range is exploitable and not what 'favors bluffing' means.

questions.question: Why do you include MORE bluffs (proportionally) when overbetting than when betting small on the river?
options: Bet size does not affect the bluff share
Small bets need more bluffs
Overbets should never be bluffed
Larger bets give the caller worse odds, so a higher bluff share keeps them indifferent

explanations.A: Bet size directly changes the indifference bluff fraction.
explanations.B: Small bets carry a smaller, not larger, bluff share.
explanations.C: Overbets are bluffed at a HIGHER rate when balanced.
explanations.D: Correct. The balanced bluff fraction B/(P+2B) rises with size, so overbets carry a higher bluff percentage.

questions.question: The single most important variable determining whether a pure bluff is +EV is:
options: How often the opponent will fold relative to the break-even threshold
Whether you feel confident
The suit of your cards
How many chips you have left

explanations.A: Correct. A pure bluff's EV hinges on fold frequency versus the break-even point set by bet size.
explanations.B: Confidence does not change the underlying math.
explanations.C: Card suits matter only via blockers, not directly to the fold-rate math.
explanations.D: Remaining stack affects sizing options but is not the core EV driver of a single bluff.

questions.question: Consider a flop semi-bluff shove with a flush draw worth ~35% when called. If villain folds 40% of the time, the bet:
options: Can only win when villain folds
Wins outright 40% AND wins ~35% of the remaining 60%, making it very likely +EV
Is identical to shoving 7-2 offsuit
Has no equity when called

explanations.A: Unlike a pure bluff, the draw also wins a share when called.
explanations.B: Correct. The shove wins 40% via folds and roughly 35% of the called 60%, stacking two paths to profit.
explanations.C: 7-2 offsuit has almost no equity when called, unlike a flush draw.
explanations.D: A flush draw has substantial equity when called, about 35%.

questions.question: You hold A♥ K♥ on Q♥ 7♠ 3♦ (nut flush draw + two overcards) after c-betting and getting called. The turn is the 2♥, completing your flush. The correct categorization of a turn bet now is:
options: A pure bluff
A semi-bluff
A value bet — your hand is now the nut flush, not a bluff
A check-fold

explanations.A: You hold the nuts, so it cannot be a pure bluff.
explanations.B: A semi-bluff implies an unmade draw; your flush is already made.
explanations.C: Correct. With the flush completed, betting is for value; it is no longer a bluff or semi-bluff.
explanations.D: Check-folding the nut flush would forfeit huge value.

questions.question: Fold equity and pot equity together determine a bet's total EV. A pure bluff relies almost entirely on:
options: Position alone
Pot equity when called
Rake
Fold equity

explanations.A: Position helps but is not the source of a pure bluff's EV.
explanations.B: Pot equity when called is near zero for a pure bluff.
explanations.C: Rake is a cost, not a source of bluff EV.
explanations.D: Correct. With negligible showdown equity, a pure bluff's EV comes from fold equity.

questions.question: A pure bluff is +EV in the long run precisely when the opponent's fold frequency is:
options: Greater than bet/(bet+pot)
Less than bet/(bet+pot)
Exactly 50% always
Equal to the MDF

explanations.A: Correct. The bluff profits whenever folds exceed the break-even threshold bet/(bet+pot).
explanations.B: Folding less than the threshold makes the bluff -EV.
explanations.C: The threshold is 50% only for a pot-sized bet, not always.
explanations.D: MDF (1 − threshold) is the defender's continuing frequency, not the bluffer's profit point.

questions.question: A coherent bluffing line on a board of A♣ Q♦ 5♠ 5♦ 9♣ representing trip fives is hard to tell if you:
options: Limped a wide range containing many fives
Raised preflop and only barreled big after the board paired, with a range that rarely contains a five
Called preflop in the big blind with fives in range
Have a credible reason to hold a five

explanations.A: A wide limping range plausibly contains fives, making the story believable.
explanations.B: Correct. A tight preflop raising range rarely contains a five, so representing trips is not credible and gets called.
explanations.C: A big-blind defending range can include fives, supporting the rep.
explanations.D: If you can credibly hold a five, the trips story is sound.

questions.question: A subtle danger of choosing a bluff that holds too many of villain's missed draws is that you:
options: Guarantee a win
Always get more folds
Block hands that would have folded, so villain's remaining range is stronger and calls more
Improve your equity when called

explanations.A: No bluff is guaranteed, especially one with poor unblocking.
explanations.B: Blocking folds reduces, not increases, the folds you get.
explanations.C: Correct. Holding their folding hands removes folds and leaves a stronger calling range, hurting your bluff.
explanations.D: Blocker selection for folds does not change your equity when called.

questions.question: A check-back in position on the flop can set up a future bluff because it:
options: Forces villain to fold immediately
Always gives villain the nuts
Forfeits all future fold equity
Disguises your range and lets you stab later when villain shows weakness

explanations.A: A check does not force a fold; it sets up later pressure.
explanations.B: Checking back does not give villain any specific hand.
explanations.C: It preserves, not forfeits, later fold equity by keeping options open.
explanations.D: Correct. Checking back keeps medium hands and bluffs in your range, enabling profitable turn/river stabs.

questions.question: Why are semi-bluffs sometimes preferred over slowplaying a strong draw?
options: Aggression adds fold equity and denies free cards, often beating a passive line
Slowplaying always wins more
Draws can never be bet
Semi-bluffs remove your equity

explanations.A: Correct. Betting the draw combines fold equity with equity realization, usually outperforming a purely passive line.
explanations.B: Slowplaying forfeits fold equity and lets opponents draw cheaply.
explanations.C: Betting draws is standard and effective.
explanations.D: Semi-bluffing keeps your draw equity intact while adding fold equity.

questions.question: Against an opponent who folds too much to RIVER bets specifically, the best exploit is to:
options: Stop bluffing rivers
Increase river bluffing frequency and size to attack their over-folding
Only value bet rivers
Check every river

explanations.A: Stopping river bluffs ignores their exploitable leak.
explanations.B: Correct. River over-folders hand you fold equity, so bluff rivers more and larger to exploit them.
explanations.C: Only value betting forfeits the profit from their over-folding.
explanations.D: Checking every river surrenders the chance to attack their folds.

questions.question: Choosing bluff sizing to match how you'd bet your value hands in the same spot is called:
options: Limp-reraising
Sandbagging
Balancing your sizing so bluffs and value are indistinguishable
Donk betting

explanations.A: Limp-reraising is a specific preflop line, not a sizing-balance concept.
explanations.B: Sandbagging is slowplaying a strong hand, unrelated to sizing balance.
explanations.C: Correct. Matching value and bluff sizes keeps your range balanced and unreadable.
explanations.D: Donk betting is leading out of position, not about size balance.

questions.question: MDF assumes the bettor can bluff with hands that have NO equity. Why does real-world defense often differ?
options: Defenders should always defend far above MDF
MDF is always exactly correct
Bluffs never have equity in practice
Bettors' bluffs frequently have equity (draws), so defenders can fold slightly more than MDF suggests

explanations.A: Over-defending well above MDF is usually a leak, not a rule.
explanations.B: MDF is an idealized guideline that real ranges modify.
explanations.C: Many bluffs are semi-bluffs with real equity.
explanations.D: Correct. When bluffs carry equity, the defender's bluff-catchers are worth less, allowing slightly more folding than pure MDF.

questions.question: Why is an ace on the turn a classic barreling card for the preflop raiser?
options: The raiser holds aces more often, so it credibly improves their range and scares the caller
Aces always make a flush
The caller always has an ace
Aces are blanks

explanations.A: Correct. The preflop raiser's range is ace-heavy, so an ace turn is a believable scare card that pressures the caller.
explanations.B: An ace does not by itself make a flush.
explanations.C: The caller does not always hold an ace; that is the point of the scare.
explanations.D: An ace is a high, range-shifting card, not a blank.

questions.question: A bluff that 'gives up' on the river by checking back can still be best because:
options: Checking always wins the pot
When fold equity is gone, checking realizes any small showdown equity and saves chips
You must always bet the river
Giving up is never correct

explanations.A: Checking does not guarantee winning the pot.
explanations.B: Correct. Without fold equity, checking captures any residual showdown chance and avoids losing more by betting into calls.
explanations.C: You are not obligated to bet every river; checking is a valid choice.
explanations.D: Giving up is frequently the highest-EV decision when bluffs stop working.

questions.question: Which draw is the best PURE semi-bluffing draw because it has both the most outs and nut potential?
options: Gutshot only
Bottom pair, no draw
Nut flush draw plus open-ended straight draw
Two undercards

explanations.A: A gutshot alone has only 4 outs, far fewer than a combo draw.
explanations.B: Bottom pair with no draw has minimal semi-bluff upside.
explanations.C: Correct. A nut flush draw plus open-ender has up to 15 outs and makes the nuts when it hits — the premier semi-bluff.
explanations.D: Two undercards have little equity and no draw.

questions.question: A bettor who is perfectly balanced on the river makes a bluff-catcher:
options: Unable to ever call
Always profit by calling
Always profit by folding
Indifferent between calling and folding

explanations.A: Balanced play does not forbid calling; it just makes it break-even.
explanations.B: If calling always profited, the bettor would be over-bluffing, not balanced.
explanations.C: If folding always profited, the bettor would be under-bluffing.
explanations.D: Correct. Balanced value-to-bluff ratios set the bluff-catcher's EV of calling equal to folding — indifference.

questions.question: Why is a semi-bluff often preferable to a pure bluff specifically in multiway pots?
options: The backup equity matters more when fold equity is split among several opponents
Pure bluffs always work multiway
Semi-bluffs have no equity
Multiway pots increase fold equity

explanations.A: Correct. With low multiway fold equity, the draw's equity provides the main source of EV, favoring semi-bluffs.
explanations.B: Pure bluffs are especially weak multiway.
explanations.C: Semi-bluffs are defined by having backup equity.
explanations.D: Multiway pots reduce, not increase, fold equity.

questions.question: On a monotone flop (three of one suit), a player WITHOUT a card of that suit bluffing should be cautious because:
options: Monotone boards always fold everyone
They cannot represent or hold the flush and lack the blocker, so fold equity drops
Flushes are impossible on monotone boards
They are guaranteed the nuts

explanations.A: Monotone boards give many players flushes and flush draws that continue.
explanations.B: Correct. Without a suit card you neither block the flush nor credibly represent it, weakening the bluff.
explanations.C: Flushes are very much possible — even already made — on monotone boards.
explanations.D: Lacking a suit card does not give you the nuts.

questions.question: A bluff's fold equity is dynamic across streets. It generally peaks when:
options: Many players remain to act
The opponent has already committed most chips
You apply pressure on a card that meaningfully threatens the opponent's range
The board has bricked all your representable hands

explanations.A: More players reduce the chance everyone folds.
explanations.B: A committed opponent folds less, lowering fold equity.
explanations.C: Correct. Fold equity is highest when a scare card credibly threatens the opponent's holdings.
explanations.D: If you can no longer represent strength, fold equity falls.

questions.question: Two bluffs win the same pot but one risks half-pot and the other risks pot. Compared to the half-pot bluff, the pot-sized bluff:
options: Can never be profitable
Needs fewer folds to break even
Has the same break-even fold frequency
Needs the opponent to fold more often but wins/keeps the same pot when it works

explanations.A: A pot-sized bluff is profitable whenever folds exceed 50%.
explanations.B: Larger bets require MORE folds to break even, not fewer.
explanations.C: The two sizes have different break-even fold frequencies.
explanations.D: Correct. The larger bet's break-even fold rate (50%) exceeds the half-pot's (33%), so it needs more folds.

questions.question: When called on a flop semi-bluff, your realized equity may be LOWER than your raw equity because:
options: You can face further bets that make you fold before realizing your draw
Raw equity always equals realized equity
Being called increases your outs
Draws always realize 100% of equity

explanations.A: Correct. Future aggression can force you off the draw, so realized equity falls below the raw figure.
explanations.B: Realized equity often differs from raw equity, especially out of position.
explanations.C: Being called does not add outs.
explanations.D: Draws frequently realize less than their full equity.

questions.question: On A♠ K♠ 9♦ 4♠ 2♣, you bluff the river representing the nut flush. The best blocker hand is:
options: K♥ K♦ (no spade)
Q♠ J♦ (holds a high spade reducing nut/strong flushes)
T♣ 9♥ (no spade)
A♦ A♥ (no spade)

explanations.A: Pocket kings holds no spade and blocks none of villain's flushes.
explanations.B: Correct. Holding the Q♠ removes strong spade-flush combos from villain's range, improving fold equity (the A♠/K♠ are on the board).
explanations.C: T-9 with no spade blocks no flush combos.
explanations.D: Pocket aces holds no spade here and does not block flushes.

questions.question: Consistent bet sizing across streets helps a triple-barrel bluff because:
options: Sizing is invisible to opponents
Bigger and bigger random bets are more convincing
It mirrors how value hands are bet, keeping the story believable
Only the flop size matters for the story

explanations.A: Opponents read sizing closely; it is far from invisible.
explanations.B: Random escalating bets break the pattern of credible value lines.
explanations.C: Correct. When bluff sizing matches value sizing, opponents cannot distinguish the two and the story holds.
explanations.D: Sizing on every street contributes to a coherent narrative.

questions.question: A 'snap bluff' fired instantly is dangerous mostly because:
options: Snap actions guarantee folds
Fast actions are against the rules
Speed always increases fold equity
Acting without considering range, board, and fold equity often turns +EV spots into -EV bluffs

explanations.A: No bluff guarantees folds, snap or otherwise.
explanations.B: Acting quickly is legal.
explanations.C: Speed by itself does not raise fold equity.
explanations.D: Correct. Bluffing requires assessing fold equity and story; skipping that analysis leads to spots where the bluff loses.

questions.question: A hand with a pair plus a flush draw (e.g., bottom pair + flush draw) semi-bluffing has equity from:
options: Both the flush outs and the chance the pair is already/becomes best
Only the flush completing
No outs at all
Solely fold equity

explanations.A: Correct. Pair-plus-draw combines flush outs with the pair's showdown potential, giving robust equity when called.
explanations.B: The pair adds equity beyond just the flush.
explanations.C: Such a hand clearly has multiple outs.
explanations.D: Beyond fold equity, the hand has real pot equity when called.

questions.question: A river-betting range that is 50% bluffs and 50% value with a pot-sized bet is:
options: Perfectly balanced
Over-bluffed — bluff-catchers can profitably call
Under-bluffed
Pure value

explanations.A: Balance for a pot bet is 2:1 value-to-bluff (33% bluffs), not 50%.
explanations.B: Correct. A balanced pot-sized bet wants ~33% bluffs; 50% is too many, so calls become profitable.
explanations.C: 50% bluffs is more than balanced, the opposite of under-bluffed.
explanations.D: Half the range being bluffs is far from pure value.

questions.question: You barrel a flush draw on the turn and miss on a brick river. With no showdown value and a sticky opponent, the standard line is to:
options: Min-bet to induce a fold
Always fire a large third barrel
Give up and check, since fold equity is low and you have no equity left
Open-fold

explanations.A: A min-bet still risks chips with poor fold equity and weak representation.
explanations.B: Firing into a sticky opponent with no equity burns chips.
explanations.C: Correct. Against a sticky player on a brick, a missed draw has little fold equity and zero showdown value, so checking is best.
explanations.D: You cannot fold when it is your turn to check or bet with no bet facing you.

questions.question: Increasing the number of streets you can credibly barrel increases total fold equity because:
options: Fold equity is fixed preflop
Barrels reduce fold equity each street
Only one street can ever generate folds
Each additional barrel gives another chance for the opponent to fold

explanations.A: Fold equity changes with each street and action, not fixed preflop.
explanations.B: Credible barrels add, not subtract, fold-equity opportunities.
explanations.C: Folds can occur on any street you bet.
explanations.D: Correct. More credible barrels mean more opportunities to make the opponent fold across the hand.

questions.question: If a defender wants to make any-two-card bluffing break-even against a half-pot bet, they must defend (not fold) about:
options: 67% of their range
33% of their range
50% of their range
80% of their range

explanations.A: Correct. MDF vs a half-pot bet = pot/(pot+bet) = 1/1.5 ≈ 67%.
explanations.B: 33% is the maximum they may FOLD, the complement of MDF.
explanations.C: 50% defense corresponds to a pot-sized bet.
explanations.D: 80% defense corresponds to a quarter-pot bet.

questions.question: The defining feature that makes a bet a SEMI-bluff rather than a value bet is that:
options: You only want calls from worse hands
You expect better hands to fold AND you still hold a meaningful draw if called
You already have the best hand
You have no equity whatsoever

explanations.A: Wanting calls from worse hands describes a value bet.
explanations.B: Correct. A semi-bluff bets a not-yet-best hand, wanting folds from better hands while retaining draw equity.
explanations.C: If you already had the best hand it would be value, not a semi-bluff.
explanations.D: A semi-bluff specifically does have equity (the draw).

questions.question: Putting it together: a profitable bluffing strategy ultimately requires that, across all your bluffs, the fold equity (plus any backup equity for semi-bluffs) exceeds:
options: The size of the blinds only
Zero in all cases regardless of sizing
The break-even threshold set by your bet sizing
100% fold frequency

explanations.A: Blinds are unrelated to the postflop break-even threshold.
explanations.B: The bar is set by sizing, not a flat zero.
explanations.C: Correct. A bluff profits when realized fold equity (plus draw equity if any) clears the bet/(bet+pot) break-even bar.
explanations.D: You never need 100% folds; you need to beat the break-even fraction.
